6 min read
Just now

--

Recent headlines about Meta’s alleged use of “AI token usage” in layoffs offer a stark warning. Here’s a practical playbook for turning AI into a genuine advantage — measured by impact, not inputs.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A few days ago, a LinkedIn post by Kevin French went viral for highlighting something many have suspected: some organizations are measuring AI “adoption” in the dumbest possible way — by counting tokens typed into chatbots rather than the value those interactions actually create.
The context is a real lawsuit. Twenty-six current and former Meta employees filed in federal court in Oakland, California, alleging that the company used internal AI systems — including AI token-usage dashboards, keystroke/activity monitoring, productivity scores, and algorithmically assisted performance rankings — to help select employees for its May 2026 layoffs (part of a roughly 8,000-person, 10% workforce reduction).
Plaintiffs claim the metrics disproportionately hurt people on protected medical, parental, or family leave — because you can’t accumulate token counts or activity data while you’re out. Meta has stated that “workforce management and organizational decisions were and are made by people, not AI,” but the complaint argues the underlying data and scoring systems baked in structural disadvantages.
The deeper point French (and many observers) made: We’re counting who’s using AI, but we’re not counting what comes out the other side.
This is the core challenge employees face right now.
The “Adoption Theater” Problem
Companies are under enormous pressure — from boards, investors, and competitors — to show they’re “AI-first.” Dashboards tracking logins, prompts sent, or tokens consumed feel like easy proof points. They’re quantifiable. They look good in a slide deck.
But they create terrible incentives:

• Gaming the system (writing longer, lower-value prompts just to hit numbers).

• Penalizing people who use AI efficiently (fewer, higher-quality interactions).

• Disadvantaging anyone with legitimate reasons for lower activity (leave, focused deep work, different role demands).

• Missing the actual business impact (faster shipping, better decisions, new revenue, reduced costs).
Token usage is an input metric. It tells you almost nothing about whether AI helped someone ship better work, make smarter decisions, or create more customer value.
How Employees Should Navigate AI at Work
Here’s a practical framework for thriving in this environment — whether your company has good metrics, bad ones, or none at all.
1. Shift from “Using AI” to “Creating Impact with AI”

• Stop optimizing for visibility. Optimize for outcomes you can prove.

• Track before/after on real work: time to complete tasks, quality of output, number of iterations needed, stakeholder feedback, or downstream metrics (bug rates, customer satisfaction, revenue influenced).

• Build a personal “AI Impact Portfolio.” For every significant project, note: “Used AI-assisted loops to reduce research time from 12 hours to 3 hours while improving source diversity; result was a strategy document that directly informed Q3 roadmap decisions.”

• In performance reviews and promotion discussions, lead with value created, not tools used.
2. Move Beyond Basic Prompting to High-Leverage Workflows

• The employees who will stand out aren’t the ones who type the most. They’re the ones who design effective systems — what some are calling loop engineering or agentic workflows.

• Treat AI as a junior teammate or research assistant that can iterate.

• Build repeatable processes: plan → execute → critique → refine.

• Learn to combine tools (search, code execution, memory, multiple models) for complex tasks.

• Focus on areas where AI multiplies your unique strengths (domain expertise + judgment) rather than replacing commodity output.
The goal is leverage, not volume.
3. Be Transparent and Ethical
• Disclose meaningful AI assistance in deliverables where it matters (code reviews, reports, creative work, customer communications).
• Don’t use AI to mask skill gaps or fabricate work. Over-reliance without understanding erodes your own capabilities over time.
• Understand your company’s AI usage policy. Some organizations now require disclosure; others monitor more than employees realize.
4. Advocate for Better Measurement (Upward and Sideways)

• You don’t control company dashboards, but you can influence what gets valued.

• In 1:1s and team meetings, ask: “How are we measuring whether AI is actually helping us ship faster/better/cheaper — not just whether people are logging in?”

• Suggest simple outcome proxies: cycle time reduction, feature velocity with quality maintained, customer NPS on AI-augmented support, internal tool adoption correlated with business KPIs.

• Share your own impact data. When enough individuals do this, it becomes harder for vanity metrics to dominate.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

5. Protect Yourself in an Uncertain Environment
Layoffs remain common across tech. AI metrics (good or bad) are now part of the data mix in many RIF processes.

• Maintain a clear record of your contributions with AI — not just activity.

• Keep strong human relationships and visibility. AI amplifies output; networks and reputation still determine who gets protected or recommended.

• Understand what data your company is collecting. Ask (politely) about performance and AI-related metrics used in reviews or workforce planning.

• Continue building portable skills. The people who thrive long-term treat AI as a skill multiplier, not a crutch.
6. Set Personal Boundaries and Balance

• AI is addictive because it feels productive. But constant context-switching between tools can reduce deep thinking.

• Block focused time where AI is off-limits or used only for specific high-value tasks.

• Use AI heavily for research, drafting, analysis, and iteration — then apply human judgment for final decisions, relationships, and creative direction.

• Protect your domain expertise. The most valuable professionals in the AI era will be those who deeply understand their field and know how to direct AI within it.
What Good Companies Are (or Should Be) Doing
The best organizations are moving past “adoption theater” toward AI maturity models that track:

• Breadth of use across roles

• Depth of integration into workflows

• Measurable business outcomes (productivity, quality, innovation, cost)

• Risk and governance (bias, data leakage, over-reliance)
They pair AI tools with training, clear policies, and outcome-based incentives rather than raw usage targets.
If your company is still stuck on token counts or login metrics, you have an opportunity to help shift the conversation — or at least insulate your own evaluation.
The Bigger Picture
We’re still early. As AI becomes more agentic — capable of running multi-step loops, maintaining context across sessions, and collaborating with other agents — the gap between “people who use AI” and “people who orchestrate AI effectively” will widen dramatically.
The Meta story is a cautionary tale about bad measurement. But it’s also a reminder: the employees who will be hardest to replace aren’t the heaviest users. They’re the ones who consistently turn AI into shipped value, better decisions, and real leverage.
Use AI. Don’t let it use you — or define you.
Focus on impact. Document outcomes. Build workflows that compound. Advocate for sensible measurement. And keep your human judgment, relationships, and unique expertise sharp.
That’s how you navigate this moment — not just survive it.
What’s your experience? Have you seen AI usage metrics (good or bad) appear in performance discussions at your company? What approaches have worked best for you personally? Share in the comments — I read and respond to thoughtful ones.
This article draws on public reporting around the Meta lawsuit and broader conversations about responsible AI adoption in enterprise settings. For the most current legal or company-specific advice, consult appropriate professionals.
